title: Triund Trek
summary: 2D/1N from Bhagsunag, Dharamshala — waterfall trail, Dhauladhar views, and camping under the stars. From ₹999.
overview:
The Triund trek in the Himalayas is a relatively easy and popular trail, offering stunning views of the snow-covered Dhauladhar ranges and Kangra Valley. It is an excellent option for anyone looking for a hassle-free weekend trek in the region. The trail’s end has a steep climb, but the overall route is short.

Triund is one of the most popular treks in India. The vast expanse near Triund Top boasts lush green meadows with deodar, oak, pine, and rhododendron forests — ideal for nature lovers. Whether you prefer a warm summer outing or a snowy winter walk, the trek is open through much of the year, except during the heavy monsoon season.

Join us for an adventure to witness nature at its finest. Book the Triund trek with friends or family this weekend and experience the magic of the Dhauladhars.

itinerary.title: Bhagsunag to Karthani (via Bhagsu Waterfall)
itinerary.accommodation: Camping at Karthani (base of Triund)
itinerary.description:
The Bhagsunag Taxi Stand is the starting point. Please arrive by 10:00 AM. The trail passes through forests of deodar, oak, fir, and rhododendron, and goes via Bhagsu waterfall and Shiva café, with panoramic views of Bhagsu village, McLeod Ganj, Dharamshala, and Kangra Valley.

From the waterfall the path becomes steeper and rockier as you climb toward Triund. The trail is marked, and small tea stalls along the way are useful for lunch breaks and water refill. In about 3–4 hours you reach Karthani — roughly 700 meters below Triund Top — for overnight camping.

If weather and forest department rules allow, enjoy sunset colours on the Dhauladhars, a campfire session, and dinner before retiring to your tent.

itinerary.title: Karthani to Triund Top & return to Bhagsunag
itinerary.accommodation: Return journey
itinerary.description:
After breakfast, hike about 1 km uphill from Karthani to Triund Top (around one hour) for sunrise views and time to explore the ridge meadows facing the Dhauladhar range.

Then descend roughly 8 km to Bhagsunag through forests and mountain landscapes, typically finishing by about 12:00 PM. Reflect on the journey as you return toward McLeod Ganj for onward travel.

Experienced and certified trekking guide
Camping gear — tents, sleeping bags, sleeping mats, kitchen utensils

exclusions: Tips / gratitude to the team

fitness.description: Triund suits beginners with basic fitness. Expect 3–5 hours of walking with a sustained uphill section. You should be comfortable on inclined trails with a light daypack.
tips: Walk 3–4 km daily for two weeks before departure
Practice stair climbs with a light backpack
Hydrate well and avoid alcohol the night before
Break in your trek shoes beforehand
Arrive at Bhagsunag by 10:00 AM on Day 1

map.overview: Start and end at Bhagsunag, Dharamshala (Kangra district). Day 1 climbs ~7 km via Bhagsu waterfall to Karthani camp. Day 2 summits Triund Top (~2,850 m) then descends ~8 km back to Bhagsunag. Reach Dharamshala by air (Gaggal), overnight bus from Delhi/Chandigarh, or train to Pathankot plus a 2–3 hour road transfer.
map.elevationNote: Max altitude ~2,850 m (9,350 ft). Steep final approach to Triund Top.

faqs.question: Is the network available in Triund?
faqs.answer: During the trek, there are spots where the cell network is accessible. When you reach the top, the network often disappears. It is best to let your family know that you may not be reachable for some time.

faqs.question: What is the best way to book a Triund Trek online?
faqs.answer: You can book the Triund Trek package directly on this website. The standard camping package starts from ₹999 per person and covers camping, guide support, dinner, and breakfast as listed in the inclusions.

faqs.question: Is camping allowed in Triund?
faqs.answer: Night camping rules can change with forest department guidelines. When permitted, camping is arranged in designated zones to manage crowd and environmental impact. Your trek leader will brief you on the approved overnight location (typically Karthani / Triund zone).

faqs.question: Can Triund trek be done in one day?
faqs.answer: Yes, Triund can be completed as a single-day hike in about 4–6 hours. For sunrise, sunset, and a night under the stars, we recommend the 2 days / 1 night camping package.

faqs.question: Is Triund Trek safe for kids?
faqs.answer: Yes, families often do this trek, but the climb can tire younger children. Be prepared to help or carry kids on steeper stretches. Share your child’s age with us when booking so we can advise accordingly.

faqs.question: Is night stay allowed in Triund?
faqs.answer: Overnight camping in the Triund area is allowed as per current state guidelines when arranged through approved operators and designated camping zones. Availability depends on weather and permits on the day of the trek.

faqs.question: Can a beginner do Triund Trek?
faqs.answer: The route has sustained uphill walking and can feel tiring for absolute first-timers, but with an easy-to-moderate grade and guide support, beginners with basic fitness regularly complete it successfully.

faqs.question: How much does the Triund trek cost?
faqs.answer: Triund Trek with camping generally starts from ₹999 per person for triple sharing. Double sharing options are available at a higher rate. Exact package prices are shown on this page.

faqs.question: Which time is best for Triund Trek?
faqs.answer: The best months are May, June, September, and October. Weather is generally pleasant, though nights can be chilly. Monsoon is scenic but less ideal for overnight camping; winter can bring snow and extra cold.

faqs.question: Is Triund Trek safe for girls?
faqs.answer: Yes. Triund is a popular and well-trafficked trail, commonly done by solo travelers and women trekkers when booking with a certified guided group.

faqs.question: Is Triund trek difficult?
faqs.answer: It is rated easy to moderate. The trek is short but includes a steep final climb. Most fit beginners complete it comfortably with paced breaks.

faqs.question: How long is a Triund Trek?
faqs.answer: The round trip is around 16 km (both ways). A day hike typically takes 4–6 hours. With camping, plan for 2 days / 1 night to enjoy sunset, night sky, and sunrise at or near Triund Top.

faqs.question: What is included in the trek fee?
faqs.answer: Evening tea, dinner and breakfast (veg), experienced certified trekking guide, camping gear (tents, sleeping bags, mats, kitchen utensils), first-aid kit with the team, and pit-style toilet tents. See the full inclusions list on this page.

faqs.question: How do I reach the starting point?
faqs.answer: Reach Dharamshala / McLeod Ganj first. The trek starts from Bhagsunag Taxi Stand. By air: Gaggal Airport. By bus: overnight coaches from Delhi (about 8–10 hours) or Chandigarh. By train: Pathankot, then a 2–3 hour bus or cab to Dharamshala. Arrive by 10:00 AM on Day 1.

faqs.question: What is your cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the tour cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available in those cases. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

title: Hampta Pass Trek
summary: Cross from lush Kullu meadows into the stark Spiti desert — one of India's most dramatic landscape switches in five days.
overview: Hampta Pass is a study in contrast. You begin among wildflowers and river valleys near Manali, climb toward a rocky alpine gateway, and descend into the moonscape silence of Spiti. Over five carefully paced days you’ll experience meadow camps, glacial streams, high wind-swept saddles, and the otherworldly beauty of Chandratal (seasonal option). Our expedition-style planning emphasizes acclimatization cues, weather discretion, and small-group attention.

title: Kheerganga Trek
summary: 2D/1N from Barshaini — Parvati Valley forests, waterfalls, camping, and a natural hot spring at the top. From ₹1,099.
overview:
Kheerganga is one of Himachal’s most loved weekend treks, tucked deep in the Parvati Valley near Kasol. People come for the hot springs at Parvati Kund and for wide views over a valley of pine, oak, and apple orchards.

The walk is about 12 km one way (roughly 24 km both ways) on a mostly gentle trail that follows the Parvati River past waterfalls and small villages. Some stretches steepen, but the overall grade suits beginners who can manage 5–7 hours of walking with a light pack.

After the climb, safari-style camping and a dip in the mineral hot springs make a classic Kasol weekend. Pair it with Tosh or Kasol cafés if you have an extra half day.

itinerary.title: Barshaini to Kheerganga
itinerary.accommodation: Safari camping at Kheerganga
itinerary.description:
Reach Barshaini — the start of the Kheerganga trek (~3,050 m). Please arrive by 10:00 AM. Stock snacks or water in the village before you leave.

The trail begins as a gentle climb past apple orchards, then moves into deodar and oak forest with the Parvati River roaring below. Expect about 12 km and 5–6 hours of walking, with waterfall and village breaks along the way.

On arrival, settle into safari tents, then soak at Parvati Kund (natural hot springs) — especially rewarding in colder months. Evening tea, dinner, and a group bonfire with music (typically till 11:00 PM when permitted) wrap up the day.

itinerary.title: Kheerganga to Barshaini
itinerary.accommodation: Return journey
itinerary.description:
Start with morning tea in the quiet of Parvati Valley. After breakfast, descend the same ~12 km trail back to Barshaini in about 4–5 hours, typically finishing by around 2:00 PM.

Pause in the villages and forest clearings for photos on the way down. Once at Barshaini, you are free to explore nearby Tosh or return to Kasol for cafés, Manikaran, or onward travel.

map.elevationNote: Max altitude ~3,050 m (10,006 ft). Modest incline with steeper mid sections.

faqs.question: Will we be able to find snow in Kheerganga?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can often see snow from late November through March. Snow is not guaranteed year-round. Winter nights stay cold. For reliable snow views in the wider region, Kasol, Tosh, and Manali are popular nearby stops.

faqs.question: Can inexperienced trekkers hike the Kheerganga trail?
faqs.answer: Yes. Kheerganga is a great first Himalayan trek for people with ordinary fitness and no prior experience. Families regularly do it; parents or guardians must accompany children under 15.

faqs.question: Is Kheerganga Trek difficult?
faqs.answer: It is rated easy to moderate. The one-way distance is about 12 km with waterfall crossings and some steeper stretches. Most people take 4–6 hours uphill. The trail is largely a forest and village walk that beginners complete successfully.

faqs.question: Is there mobile network in Kheerganga?
faqs.answer: Coverage on the trail and at the top is poor. BSNL works in patches; other networks often fail. Make important calls from Kasol or Barshaini before you start.

faqs.question: What are the risks of high-altitude trekking?
faqs.answer: Altitude can cause headache, nausea, or fatigue. Tell your trek leader if you feel unwell and carry personal medication. The team carries a basic first-aid kit; rest, hydrate, and descend if symptoms worsen.

faqs.question: What are the most important Kheerganga trekking tips?
faqs.answer: Prepare for changing weather, carry personal medicine, go with a guide if you are new to hiking, expect remote camping with limited amenities, avoid wasting food or water, follow camp rules, and stay with your group.

faqs.question: Is it advisable to take the Kheerganga trek in winter?
faqs.answer: The route stays open in winter, but snow can make sections slippery and slow. Use proper trekking shoes and poles, and prefer a guided group. The hot springs at the top feel especially rewarding after a cold climb.

faqs.question: What is Kheerganga well-known for?
faqs.answer: Kheerganga is known for natural hot springs (Parvati Kund), camping under Himalayan skies, forest trails through Parvati Valley villages, and a relaxed weekend escape from city life — often paired with time in Kasol.

faqs.question: Can we spend the night in Kheerganga?
faqs.answer: Overnight camping is arranged as part of this 2 days / 1 night package when permitted by local guidelines. After a 5–7 hour ascent, you camp near the hot springs and return the next day.

faqs.question: Where does the Kheerganga Trek begin?
faqs.answer: The trek starts at Barshaini (also spelled Bharshaini), near the Parvati dam, at roughly 1,189 m. It is a short drive from Kasol and about three hours by road from Manali. Overnight camping is at Kheerganga (~3,050 m).

faqs.question: What is the history behind the Kheerganga name?
faqs.answer: Local lore links the area with Lord Shiva and Kartikeya. “Kheerganga” refers to the milky-white sacred hot spring water. A dip in Parvati Kund is considered holy, and there is an old temple near the springs.

faqs.question: Is drinking water available on the Kheerganga trail?
faqs.answer: Carry what you need from Barshaini, as shops thin out after the start. You can refill at natural springs and ashrams along the route when available — ask your guide which sources are safe.

faqs.question: How do I get to Kheerganga / Barshaini?
faqs.answer: There is no direct rail or road from Delhi to Kasol. By road: overnight bus from Delhi ISBT Kashmere Gate / Majnu-Ka-Tila toward Kullu–Manali, alight at Bhuntar, then local bus to Kasol and cab or shared jeep to Barshaini. By rail: Pathankot or Joginder Nagar, then road to Kasol. By air: Bhuntar (Kullu) Airport, about 31 km from Kasol, then onward to Barshaini. Arrive by 10:00 AM on Day 1.

faqs.question: What places can I visit near Kheerganga?
faqs.answer: Tosh and Barshaini are scenic village stops. Kasol cafés and riverside walks are popular, Manikaran Sahib is a short hop away, and Manali is about three hours by road. Chalal and Malana make good add-on day hikes if you have extra time.

faqs.question: What is the best time to visit Kheerganga?
faqs.answer: March to June is the most popular window — pleasant days and clearer views. September to November is also excellent after the rains. Monsoon is lush but slipperier; winter brings cold and possible snow.

faqs.question: What is included in the trek fee?
faqs.answer: Day 1 evening tea and dinner, Day 2 breakfast, evening group bonfire with music (till about 11:00 PM when conditions allow), experienced guide Barshaini–Kheerganga–Barshaini, and safari camps on a triple/quad sharing basis with common washroom.

faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the tour cost. title: Bhrigu Lake Trek
summary: A high alpine lake trail above Manali with meadow camps, dramatic ridgelines, and crystal-blue glacial water.
overview: Bhrigu Lake is Manali’s alpine jewel — a compact itinerary with oversized scenery. Within a few days you trade orchards and valley roads for flower meadows, ridgelines, and a sacred high lake cradled by peaks. Ideal for trekkers seeking altitude experience without a long expedition calendar, Bhrigu rewards preparation with quiet morning light and mirror-still water when winds sleep.

title: Kareri Lake Trek
summary: 2D/1N from Kareri Village near Dharamshala — oak forests, Nyund stream, and a high freshwater alpine lake. From ₹1,499.
overview:
Kareri Lake sits at the foothills of the Dhauladhar range in Kangra Valley, about 9 km above Kareri Village. It is a high-altitude freshwater lake fed by meltwater from snowy peaks, with clear shallows where you can often see the lake bed.

The approach winds through silver oak, rhododendron, and Chir pine beside the Nyund stream. Meadows around the lake double as seasonal grazing grounds for Gaddi shepherds before they cross Minkiani and Baleni passes toward Chamba and Bharmour.

From December to March the lake frequently freezes and access may need technical mountaineering gear. In open season it is an easy-to-moderate weekend trek for fit beginners who want quieter trails than the busiest Dharamshala day hikes.

itinerary.title: Kareri Village to Kareri Lake via Riyoti
itinerary.accommodation: Camping at Kareri Lake
itinerary.description:
Meet at Kareri Village by 10:00 AM (earlier arrival helps). The trail climbs through silver oak, rhododendron, and longleaf Chir pine, then steepens along the Nyund glacial stream toward the lake (~2,934–3,100 m).

Pause for rest and packed lunch at Riyoti (also called Reoti / Liyoti) — the halfway camp. Weather decides the final push to the lake; from December to March ice and deep snow can block casual access without mounts gear.

Overnight in dome tents at Kareri Lake camp under the Dhauladhars.

itinerary.title: Kareri Lake to Kareri Village
itinerary.accommodation: Return journey
itinerary.description:
After breakfast, descend 3–4 hours to Kareri Village with the lake vistas still fresh. The trek ends here by about 2:00 PM.

If needed, your guide can help arrange a taxi back into Dharamshala or McLeod Ganj at an extra cost.

fitness.description: Kareri suits fit beginners. Expect 4–5 hours uphill with steeper rocky sections after Riyoti. Comfortable walking on forest paths with a daypack is enough for most people.
tips: Build walking stamina with 4–5 km walks before the trip
Carry a packed lunch for Day 1
Use ankle-support shoes on rocky stretches
Carry cash — network and UPI are unreliable
Arrive at Kareri Village by 10:00 AM on Day 1

map.overview: Start and end at Kareri Village near Dharamshala (Kangra). Day 1 climbs via Riyoti along the Nyund stream to Kareri Lake camp (~3,100 m). Day 2 descends to the village. Reach Dharamshala by air (Gaggal), overnight bus from Delhi/Chandigarh, or train to Pathankot plus road transfer, then cab to Kareri Village.
map.elevationNote: Max altitude ~3,100 m (10,170 ft). Steeper final approach from Riyoti.

faqs.question: Can we visit Kareri Lake in December?
faqs.answer: April to December generally works for organising the trek, covering summer, monsoon, and early winter moods. From late December through March the lake often freezes and may need technical gear — check conditions before you book a winter date.

faqs.question: Which trek is better — Triund or Kareri?
faqs.answer: Triund is busier and closer to McLeod Ganj, with classic Dhauladhar views. Kareri Lake is quieter and suits hikers who want a freshwater lake, forest trails, and fewer crowds. Many travellers do both on separate weekends.

faqs.question: Does it snow at Kareri Lake?
faqs.answer: Yes. At this altitude snow is common mid-December through February. Trails and the lake shore can be snowbound — winter visits need warmer gear and guide judgement on route safety.

faqs.question: Can Kareri Lake be reached by car?
faqs.answer: No. Drive or take a cab to Kareri Village (Kareri Khas) near Dharamshala, then begin the trek on foot. The lake itself is reached only by trail.

faqs.question: Is there network at Kareri Lake?
faqs.answer: Expect little or no coverage on the trail and at the lake. Even BSNL is intermittent. Carry cash — digital payments are unreliable once you leave the village.

faqs.question: How can I reach Kareri from Chandigarh?
faqs.answer: Buses and shared cabs run Chandigarh to Dharamshala (roughly 6–7 hours). From Dharamshala, hire a taxi to Kareri Village and start the trek by 10:00 AM on Day 1.

faqs.question: How do I go to Kareri Lake from Dharamshala?
faqs.answer: Hire a jeep or cab from Dharamshala toward Ghera / Kareri Village (about 20+ km by road depending on route). The trek starts from Kareri Village and follows the Nyund stream uphill to the lake.

faqs.question: Can I do the Kareri Lake trek alone?
faqs.answer: Solo travellers regularly join guided groups. Booking with a certified guide is safer for navigation, weather calls, and camping logistics.

faqs.question: How do you do the Kareri Trek?
faqs.answer: Reach Dharamshala first. Local buses go toward Ghera; from Ghera, Kareri Village is about a 2-hour walk, or take a cab direct to the village. Meet the group by 10:00 AM and trek via Riyoti to the lake camp.

faqs.question: How long is the trek from Kareri Village?
faqs.answer: One way is roughly 9–10 km (about 20 km both ways). Expect 4–5 hours uphill via Riyoti halfway camp, and 3–4 hours on the return.

faqs.question: Can you swim in Kareri Lake?
faqs.answer: It is a shallow freshwater lake. Some people swim in summer when the water is open; in winter the surface often freezes under deep snow on the approach — swimming is not advisable then.

faqs.question: Can we do Kareri Lake trek in one day?
faqs.answer: A same-day out-and-back is tough (roughly 20 km both ways with climb). Most people prefer 2 days / 1 night to enjoy the lakeside camp and avoid a rushed dark descent.

faqs.question: Is Kareri Lake Trek easy?
faqs.answer: It is easy to intermediate for anyone with reasonable fitness. Much of the path is straightforward, but some rocky uphill stretches demand paced walking and sturdy shoes.

faqs.question: What is included in the trek fee?
faqs.answer: Day 1 evening tea and dinner, Day 2 breakfast, certified guide, camping gear (tents, sleeping bags, mats, kitchen utensils), bonfire when available, and pit-style toilet tents. Forest or other permits are not included.

faqs.question: What is your cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the tour cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available in those cases. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

title: Snowline Laka Trek
summary: 3D/2N from Bhagsunag — Triund camp, then climb to Laka Glacier / Snowline with Indrahar Pass views. From ₹2,500.
overview:
Snowline Laka (also called Snowline or Laka Got) sits in the Dhauladhar range above Triund near McLeod Ganj. If Triund is on your list, this extension takes you closer to the snowline and the approach toward Indrahar Pass.

The trail is steeper beyond Triund and rewards you with glacier scenery, possible snow walks in season, and occasional wildlife sightings such as mountain goats. Camping nights at Triund and Laka make it a fuller weekend than a single Triund overnight.

Rated easy to moderate, the 3 days / 2 nights itinerary starts at Bhagsunag at 10:00 AM and typically finishes back there around 2:00 PM on Day 3.

itinerary.title: Bhagsunag to Triund Top
itinerary.accommodation: Camping at Triund Top
itinerary.description:
Meet at 10:00 AM at Bhagsunag Temple — the trek start. Hike toward Triund via the Bhagsu Falls route. Pause 10–20 minutes at the waterfall, then continue to the Leta midpoint to rest and eat.

Reach Triund Top campsite around 3:00 PM for ridge views of the Dhauladhars and Kangra Valley. Enjoy sunset colours, dinner, and an overnight camp at Triund.

itinerary.title: Triund to Snowline / Laka Glacier
itinerary.accommodation: Camping at Laka
itinerary.description:
Wake for sunrise and breakfast around 8:00 AM. Continue from Triund Top toward Laka Glacier / Snowline — about 6 km and usually 2–3 hours. The trail often becomes snowier as you gain height (~3,200 m).

At Laka, take in Indrahar Pass views up close. Evening bonfire and dinner around 8:00 PM (when conditions allow). Overnight at Laka Camp.

faqs.question: Is Snowline Laka Trek difficult?
faqs.answer: It is rated easy to moderate. Day 1 follows the classic Triund trail; Day 2 adds a steeper, rockier climb to Laka / Snowline (~3,200 m). Beginners with basic fitness manage it when paced with a guide.

faqs.question: Will there be snow on the Snowline / Laka trail?
faqs.answer: Snow cover increases in winter and early spring. From late autumn through March you are more likely to walk on snow patches or continuous snow near Laka Glacier. Summer may show only patches depending on the season.

faqs.question: How is this trek connected to Triund?
faqs.answer: Day 1 camps at Triund Top after the Bhagsunag–waterfall–Leta climb. Day 2 continues ~6 km beyond Triund to Laka Glacier / Snowline with views toward Indrahar Pass, then Day 3 descends all the way to Bhagsunag.

faqs.question: Where does the Snowline Laka Trek start?
faqs.answer: Meet at Bhagsunag (Bhagsu Nag), Dharamshala, by 10:00 AM on Day 1 near the temple / taxi stand area. The route goes via Bhagsu waterfall toward Triund, then onward to Laka.

faqs.question: How long is the Snowline Laka Trek?
faqs.answer: Plan for 3 days / 2 nights. Total walking is around 24 km including Triund and the Laka extension. Day 3 typically ends back at Bhagsunag around 2:00 PM.

Our winter operations prioritize route discretion, layer systems, and warm camps that still feel wild.

title: Snowline Trek
summary: Snowline Trek above Triund — climb past the popular ridge to the first permanent snow patches of the Dhauladhar with Indrahar Pass views. From ₹1,999.
overview:
The Snowline Trek is the classic next step after Triund for travellers who want a true alpine feel without committing to a full Indrahar Pass crossing. From Bhagsunag you climb through oak and rhododendron, crest the Triund ridge, then continue toward the snow-stained scree where the Dhauladhar walls rise abruptly.

On clear days the ridge reveals a sweeping line of peaks and the notch of Indrahar Pass. Winter and early spring often paint the upper path white; late autumn brings razor-clear views and cold nights. This itinerary is designed for India Holiday Destinations guests seeking a weekend-friendly Dharamshala adventure with guided camping support.

Expect a mix of forest trail, open meadow, and rocky alpine approach. The last stretch above Triund is steeper and more exposed to wind, so layering and an early start matter as much as raw fitness.

Best time to visit: March–May and September–November; winter for snow seekers

Flora along the Snowline corridor: Ban oak, rhododendron, deodar lower down; alpine scrub and sparse herbs near Snowline.

Wildlife notes for Snowline Trek: Himalayan monal and other pheasants in forest belts; occasional Himalayan goral on steep grass slopes; langurs near village edges.

Photography spots: Triund ridge looking into Kangra Valley; Snowline amphitheatre toward Indrahar; Night sky from camp when clouds clear; Golden hour on Dhauladhar ice flutings.

Camping locations: Designated Snowline camping shelf; Backup Karthani / Triund zone if weather turns.

Water sources: Carry 2L from Bhagsunag/Triund. Seasonal seeps exist but are not reliable in peak winter freeze.

Network availability: Patchy Jio/Airtel till Triund; usually none at Snowline. Inform family before leaving McLeod Ganj.

Electricity availability: No charging points on the trail. Carry a full power bank.

ATM availability: ATMs in McLeod Ganj and Dharamshala only — withdraw before the trek.

Medical facilities: Basic first aid with guide; nearest clinics in McLeod Ganj; hospital in Dharamshala.

Permits required: Forest camping permissions as applicable; arranged for booked packages when overnight camping is allowed.

Forest permissions: Overnight stays must follow Himachal forest / local authority guidelines for the Triund–Snowline corridor.

Safety on Snowline: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Acclimatise with a short walk in McLeod Ganj the evening before. Carry microspikes in late winter if ice is reported above Triund. Book weekdays if you want quieter camps than weekend Triund crowds.

Responsible tourism on Snowline Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: Bhagsu Waterfall, McLeod Ganj monasteries, Dal Lake (Naddi side), St. John in the Wilderness.

Nearby treks: Triund Trek, Laka Glacier Trek, Lahesh Cave Trek, Indrahar Pass Trek.

Child policy for Snowline Trek: 10+ years (with guardians). Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private departures available for families and small groups — custom dates subject to guide and permit availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ confirmed travellers on the same departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Warm sleeping layer; Trekking poles; Sunglasses for snow glare; Extra batteries for headlamp.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Snowline Trek?
faqs.answer: This route is rated easy. Snowline sits above Triund on steeper, rockier ground. Beginners with good stamina manage it when they start early and pace themselves.

faqs.question: How long does the Snowline Trek take?
faqs.answer: The standard package is 2D / 1N. Actual walking time varies with fitness, weather, and trail conditions.

faqs.question: What is the total trekking distance for Snowline Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect roughly 18 km of walking across the itinerary (out-and-back or point-to-point depending on the route plan).

faqs.question: What is the maximum altitude on Snowline Trek?
faqs.answer: The highest point is about 10,499 ft (3,200 m). Ascend steadily, hydrate, and tell your guide if you feel altitude-related symptoms on higher Dhauladhar routes.

faqs.question: Where does the Snowline Trek start?
faqs.answer: The usual starting point is Bhagsunag, McLeod Ganj. Arrive the evening before when the itinerary begins early.

faqs.question: When is the best time for Snowline Trek?
faqs.answer: March–May and September–November; winter for snow seekers

faqs.answer: Designated Snowline camping shelf Backup Karthani / Triund zone if weather turns

faqs.question: Can beginners attempt the Snowline Trek?
faqs.answer: It depends on the grade. Snowline sits above Triund on steeper, rockier ground. Beginners with good stamina manage it when they start early and pace themselves. If you are new to the Himalayas, choose a guided departure and prepare with regular walks carrying a daypack.

faqs.question: How do I reach the trailhead for Snowline Trek?
faqs.answer: Fly into Gaggal (DHM) or take a train to Pathankot, then road transfer to McLeod Ganj. Local taxis and shared jeeps cover the final approach to Bhagsunag, McLeod Ganj.

faqs.question: What should I pack for Snowline Trek?
faqs.answer: Carry layered clothing, broken-in trekking shoes, rain shell, headlamp, sunscreen, personal medicines, and a power bank. A detailed packing list is provided on this page and with your booking confirmation.

faqs.question: Is Snowline Trek safe for solo travellers?
faqs.answer: Guided group departures are the safest option for solo travellers in the Dhauladhar. Trails can be remote, weather changes fast, and night navigation is risky without local knowledge.

faqs.question: What is the cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the trek cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

faqs.question: Are meals included on the Snowline Trek?
faqs.answer: Trek-day meals are included as listed in the package inclusions (typically vegetarian). Confirm dietary needs when you book so the kitchen team can plan.

faqs.question: What medical facilities are near Snowline Trek?
faqs.answer: Basic clinics exist in McLeod Ganj. For serious emergencies, evacuation aims for Civil / Zonal Hospital Dharamshala. Carry personal medication and inform us of any conditions before departure.

faqs.question: Is Snowline the same as Laka Glacier?
faqs.answer: Snowline is the alpine zone above Triund where snow often persists. Laka Glacier / Laka Got sits further along the same corridor toward Indrahar Pass — a longer and colder outing.

faqs.question: Can I do Snowline as a day hike?
faqs.answer: Strong hikers sometimes tag Snowline as a long day from Bhagsunag, but overnight camping gives safer timing and better light for photography.

title: Laka Glacier Trek
summary: Continue past Snowline to Laka Got — glacial cirque, Lahesh Cave approach, and Indrahar Pass walls on a 3D/2N Dhauladhar classic. From ₹2,899.
overview:
Laka Glacier Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Continue past Snowline to Laka Got — glacial cirque, Lahesh Cave approach, and Indrahar Pass walls on a 3D/2N Dhauladhar classic.

Laka Got feels like the antechamber of Indrahar — a cold cirque where summer still holds snowfields and the wind funnels straight off the pass. Unlike busy Triund, the final approach is quiet, rocky, and unmistakably alpine.

The itinerary usually starts at Bhagsunag and ends at Bhagsunag, spanning about 22 km across 3 day(s) with a high point near 11,480 ft. Graded moderate, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Laka Glacier corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Laka Glacier Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Laka Glacier main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Laka Glacier camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Laka Glacier: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in McLeod Ganj a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Laka Glacier Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Indrahar Pass Trek, Kareri Village Trek, Minkiani Pass Trek, Kareri Lake Trek.

Child policy for Laka Glacier Trek: 12+ with fitness. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Laka Glacier Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

title: Indrahar Pass Trek
summary: Cross the iconic Indrahar Pass (≈4,342 m) on the Dhauladhar divide between Kangra and Chamba — serious altitude, scree, and huge Himalayan views. From ₹8,999.
overview:

Indrahar is the classic Kangra–Chamba gateway: scree, thin air, and a sudden reveal of Chamba’s layered ranges once you crest the notch. This is not a casual weekend extension of Triund — treat it as a proper high pass.

The itinerary usually starts at Bhagsunag / Dharamkot and ends at Chamba side or return via McLeod, spanning about 32 km across 4 day(s) with a high point near 14,250 ft. title: Kareri Village Trek
summary: A gentle cultural walk into Kareri Village — Gaddi homes, terraced fields, and the gateway trail toward Kareri Lake. From ₹999.
overview:
Kareri Village Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. A gentle cultural walk into Kareri Village — Gaddi homes, terraced fields, and the gateway trail toward Kareri Lake.

Kareri Village is where Gaddi hospitality and terraced farming meet the trailhead for the famous lake. The walk is cultural as much as scenic — stone homes, prayer flags, and the Nyund stream guiding you in.

The itinerary usually starts at Ghera / near Kareri roadhead and ends at Kareri Village, spanning about 8 km across 1 day(s) with a high point near 5,900 ft. Graded easy, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

title: Minkiani Pass Trek
summary: A demanding Dhauladhar crossing above Kareri Lake via Minkiani Pass — remote meadows, high camps, and few crowds. From ₹11,999.
overview:
Minkiani Pass Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. A demanding Dhauladhar crossing above Kareri Lake via Minkiani Pass — remote meadows, high camps, and few crowds.

Minkiani sits above the Kareri basin as a lonelier sibling to Indrahar. Expect long meadow days, cold nights, and navigation that rewards patience more than speed.

The itinerary usually starts at Kareri Village and ends at Chamba / return via Kareri, spanning about 40 km across 5 day(s) with a high point near 13,900 ft. Graded challenging, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Minkiani Pass corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Minkiani Pass Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Minkiani Pass main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Minkiani Pass camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Dharamshala a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Minkiani Pass Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

title: Bhagsu Waterfall Trek
summary: The classic McLeod Ganj waterfall walk — cafés, temple steps, and a cool plunge-pool amphitheatre under the Dhauladhar foothills. From ₹499.
overview:
Bhagsu Waterfall Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. The classic McLeod Ganj waterfall walk — cafés, temple steps, and a cool plunge-pool amphitheatre under the Dhauladhar foothills.

Bhagsu’s waterfall amphitheatre is McLeod Ganj’s most accessible nature walk: café chatter fades into spray, temple bells, and a cool basin under the foothills.

The itinerary usually starts at Bhagsunag Temple and ends at Bhagsu Waterfall, spanning about 3 km across 1 day(s) with a high point near 6,200 ft. Graded easy, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Bhagsu Waterfall corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Bhagsu Waterfall Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Bhagsu Waterfall main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Not applicable — day trek.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: Charge devices in town before starting.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Bhagsu Waterfall: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in McLeod Ganj a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Bhagsu Waterfall Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Dharamkot Trek, Naddi Trek, Dal Lake Trail, Triund Trek.

Child policy for Bhagsu Waterfall Trek: 8+ with guardians. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Bhagsu Waterfall Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

title: Dal Lake Trail
summary: A peaceful forest-and-meadow stroll to Dharamshala’s Dal Lake — deodars, local picnics, and soft hills under the high Dhauladhar wall. From ₹499.
overview:
Dal Lake Trail is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. A peaceful forest-and-meadow stroll to Dharamshala’s Dal Lake — deodars, local picnics, and soft hills under the high Dhauladhar wall.

Dal Lake near Naddi is a deodar-framed pause button: locals picnic, prayer flags stir, and the high ridge hangs like a painted backdrop above the water.

The itinerary usually starts at Naddi / Forsyth Ganj and ends at Dal Lake, spanning about 3 km across 1 day(s) with a high point near 6,000 ft. Graded easy, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Dal Lake corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Dal Lake Trail: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Dal Lake main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Not applicable — day trek.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: Charge devices in town before starting.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Dal Lake: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Naddi a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Dal Lake Trail: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp, Hanuman Ka Tibba Expedition, Moon Peak Trek, Triund Trek.

Child policy for Dal Lake Trail: 8+ with guardians. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Dal Lake Trail departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

title: Hanuman Ka Tibba Expedition
summary: Guided mountaineering expedition aiming for Hanuman Ka Tibba — ropework, glacier travel, summit window, and full expedition support. From ₹45,999.
overview:
Hanuman Ka Tibba Expedition is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Guided mountaineering expedition aiming for Hanuman Ka Tibba — ropework, glacier travel, summit window, and full expedition support.

The full Hanuman Ka Tibba expedition mixes rope skills, glacier travel, and a narrow summit window. Only experienced climbers with guided support should consider it.

The itinerary usually starts at Manali / Solang / designated BC and ends at Summit attempt & descend, spanning about 70 km across 8 day(s) with a high point near 19,300 ft. Graded difficult, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: July–September weather windows only; avoid deep winter

Flora along the Hanuman Ka Tibba corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Hanuman Ka Tibba Expedition: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Hanuman Ka Tibba main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Hanuman Ka Tibba camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Hanuman Ka Tibba: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Manali–Dhauladhar approaches a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Hanuman Ka Tibba Expedition: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Moon Peak Trek, Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek, Lam Dal Trek, Snowline Laka Trek.

Child policy for Hanuman Ka Tibba Expedition: 16+ experienced preferred. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Hanuman Ka Tibba Expedition departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

title: Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek
summary: Multi-day camping under the main Dhauladhar wall — meadows, high pastures, and a non-pass base-camp style itinerary for photographers and ridge lovers. From ₹7,999.
overview:
Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Multi-day camping under the main Dhauladhar wall — meadows, high pastures, and a non-pass base-camp style itinerary for photographers and ridge lovers.

This base-camp style itinerary prioritises meadow camping under the main wall — ideal for photographers who want dawn light without committing to a pass crossing.

The itinerary usually starts at Bhagsunag or Kareri (route dependent) and ends at High meadow base camp, spanning about 30 km across 4 day(s) with a high point near 12,500 ft. Graded moderate, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Dhauladhar Base Camp corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Dhauladhar Base Camp main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Dhauladhar Base Camp camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Dhauladhar Base Camp: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Dharamshala a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Lam Dal Trek, Saat Dal Trek, Nag Dal Trek, Kareri Lake Trek.

Child policy for Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek: 12+ with fitness. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

Video and extended gallery placeholders are reserved for Website Management CMS uploads.

Fixed departure calendar and live pricing shown in the booking panel remain editable from CMS.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek?
faqs.answer: This route is rated moderate. The Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek demands moderate effort across 4 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 12500 ft.

faqs.question: How long does the Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek take?
faqs.answer: The standard package is 4D / 3N. Actual walking time varies with fitness, weather, and trail conditions.

faqs.question: What is the total trekking distance for Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect roughly 30 km of walking across the itinerary (out-and-back or point-to-point depending on the route plan).

faqs.question: What is the maximum altitude on Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek?
faqs.answer: The highest point is about 12,500 ft (3,810 m). Ascend steadily, hydrate, and tell your guide if you feel altitude-related symptoms on higher Dhauladhar routes.

faqs.question: Where does the Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek start?
faqs.answer: The usual starting point is Bhagsunag or Kareri (route dependent). Arrive the evening before when the itinerary begins early.

faqs.question: When is the best time for Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek?
faqs.answer: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

faqs.question: Is mobile network available on Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

faqs.question: Do I need permits for Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek?
faqs.answer: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

faqs.question: Is drinking water available on the Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek trail?
faqs.answer: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

faqs.question: Where do we camp or stay on Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek?
faqs.answer: Designated Dhauladhar Base Camp camping zones Backup lower camp in bad weather

faqs.question: Can beginners attempt the Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek?
faqs.answer: It depends on the grade. The Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek demands moderate effort across 4 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 12500 ft. If you are new to the Himalayas, choose a guided departure and prepare with regular walks carrying a daypack.

faqs.question: How do I reach the trailhead for Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek?
faqs.answer: Fly into Gaggal (DHM) or take a train to Pathankot, then road transfer to Dharamshala. Local taxis and shared jeeps cover the final approach to Bhagsunag or Kareri (route dependent).

faqs.question: What should I pack for Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek?
faqs.answer: Carry layered clothing, broken-in trekking shoes, rain shell, headlamp, sunscreen, personal medicines, and a power bank. A detailed packing list is provided on this page and with your booking confirmation.

faqs.question: Is Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek safe for solo travellers?
faqs.answer: Guided group departures are the safest option for solo travellers in the Dhauladhar. Trails can be remote, weather changes fast, and night navigation is risky without local knowledge.

faqs.question: What is the cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the trek cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

faqs.question: Are meals included on the Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek?
faqs.answer: Trek-day meals are included as listed in the package inclusions (typically vegetarian). Confirm dietary needs when you book so the kitchen team can plan.

faqs.question: What medical facilities are near Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek?
faqs.answer: Basic clinics exist in Dharamshala. For serious emergencies, evacuation aims for Civil / Zonal Hospital Dharamshala. Carry personal medication and inform us of any conditions before departure.

faqs.question: Is Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek crowded?
faqs.answer: Dhauladhar Base Camp sees far fewer groups than Triund — expect quieter camps and longer self-sufficiency.

faqs.question: Can I combine Dhauladhar Base Camp with Triund?
faqs.answer: Many travellers pair easier Dharamshala walks with Triund or Kareri Lake on separate days. Ask us for a custom multi-trek plan.

title: Lam Dal Trek
summary: Pilgrim-meets-alpine trek to sacred Lam Dal in the Dhauladhar–Manimahesh hinterland — high meadows, glacial lakes, and remote camps. From ₹15,999.
overview:

Lam Dal blends pilgrimage energy with glacial-lake silence. High pastures, cold water, and remote camps define the approach from the Bharmour hinterland.

The itinerary usually starts at Bharmour / Hadsar region and ends at Lam Dal alpine lake, spanning about 48 km across 6 day(s) with a high point near 12,800 ft. Graded challenging, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Saat Dal Trek?
faqs.answer: This route is rated challenging. The Saat Dal Trek demands challenging effort across 7 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 13200 ft.

faqs.question: How long does the Saat Dal Trek take?
faqs.answer: The standard package is 7D / 6N. Actual walking time varies with fitness, weather, and trail conditions.

faqs.question: What is the total trekking distance for Saat Dal Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect roughly 55 km of walking across the itinerary (out-and-back or point-to-point depending on the route plan).

faqs.question: What is the maximum altitude on Saat Dal Trek?
faqs.answer: The highest point is about 13,200 ft (4,023 m). Ascend steadily, hydrate, and tell your guide if you feel altitude-related symptoms on higher Dhauladhar routes.

faqs.question: Where does the Saat Dal Trek start?
faqs.answer: The usual starting point is Bharmour region roadhead. Arrive the evening before when the itinerary begins early.

faqs.question: When is the best time for Saat Dal Trek?
faqs.answer: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

faqs.question: Is mobile network available on Saat Dal Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

faqs.question: Do I need permits for Saat Dal Trek?
faqs.answer: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

faqs.question: Is drinking water available on the Saat Dal Trek trail?
faqs.answer: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

faqs.question: Where do we camp or stay on Saat Dal Trek?
faqs.answer: Designated Saat Dal camping zones Backup lower camp in bad weather

faqs.question: Can beginners attempt the Saat Dal Trek?
faqs.answer: It depends on the grade. The Saat Dal Trek demands challenging effort across 7 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 13200 ft. If you are new to the Himalayas, choose a guided departure and prepare with regular walks carrying a daypack.

faqs.question: How do I reach the trailhead for Saat Dal Trek?
faqs.answer: Fly into Gaggal (DHM) or take a train to Pathankot, then road transfer to Chamba hinterland. Local taxis and shared jeeps cover the final approach to Bharmour region roadhead.

faqs.question: What should I pack for Saat Dal Trek?
faqs.answer: Carry layered clothing, broken-in trekking shoes, rain shell, headlamp, sunscreen, personal medicines, and a power bank. A detailed packing list is provided on this page and with your booking confirmation.

faqs.question: Is Saat Dal Trek safe for solo travellers?
faqs.answer: Guided group departures are the safest option for solo travellers in the Dhauladhar. Trails can be remote, weather changes fast, and night navigation is risky without local knowledge.

faqs.question: What is the cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the trek cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

faqs.question: Are meals included on the Saat Dal Trek?
faqs.answer: Trek-day meals are included as listed in the package inclusions (typically vegetarian). Confirm dietary needs when you book so the kitchen team can plan.

faqs.question: What medical facilities are near Saat Dal Trek?
faqs.answer: Basic clinics exist in Chamba hinterland. For serious emergencies, evacuation aims for Civil / Zonal Hospital Dharamshala. Carry personal medication and inform us of any conditions before departure.

faqs.question: Is Saat Dal Trek crowded?
faqs.answer: Saat Dal sees far fewer groups than Triund — expect quieter camps and longer self-sufficiency.

faqs.question: Can I combine Saat Dal with Triund?
faqs.answer: Many travellers pair easier Dharamshala walks with Triund or Kareri Lake on separate days. Ask us for a custom multi-trek plan.

title: Nag Dal Trek
summary: High-altitude lake trek to Nag Dal — serpentine meadows, icy shores, and a quieter alternative to busier Himachal lake trails. From ₹13,999.
overview:
Nag Dal Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. High-altitude lake trek to Nag Dal — serpentine meadows, icy shores, and a quieter alternative to busier Himachal lake trails.

Nag Dal stays quieter than Himachal’s poster lakes. Expect icy shores, long approaches, and a strong sense of being deep in shepherd country.

The itinerary usually starts at Local roadhead near Bharmour belt and ends at Nag Dal, spanning about 42 km across 5 day(s) with a high point near 12,600 ft. Graded challenging, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Nag Dal corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Nag Dal Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Nag Dal main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Nag Dal camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Nag Dal: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Chamba / Dhauladhar a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Nag Dal Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Kali Kund Trek, Lahesh Cave Trek, Gaj Pass Trek, Kareri Lake Trek.

Child policy for Nag Dal Trek: 16+ experienced preferred. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Nag Dal Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

title: Kali Kund Trek

title: Lahesh Cave Trek
summary: Trek beyond Snowline to the legendary Lahesh Cave — natural rock shelter, Indrahar staging ground, and one of Dharamshala’s most atmospheric camps. From ₹3,499.
overview:
Lahesh Cave Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Trek beyond Snowline to the legendary Lahesh Cave — natural rock shelter, Indrahar staging ground, and one of Dharamshala’s most atmospheric camps.

Lahesh Cave is the storied rock shelter on the Indrahar approach: wind-scoured, photogenic, and a natural overnight for parties pushing beyond Snowline.

The itinerary usually starts at Bhagsunag and ends at Lahesh Cave / return, spanning about 24 km across 3 day(s) with a high point near 11,500 ft. Graded moderate, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Lahesh Cave corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Lahesh Cave Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Lahesh Cave main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Lahesh Cave camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Lahesh Cave: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in McLeod Ganj a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Lahesh Cave Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Gaj Pass Trek, Chobia Pass Trek, Kugti Pass Trek, Triund Trek.

Child policy for Lahesh Cave Trek: 12+ with fitness. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Lahesh Cave Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

summary: High Chobia Pass expedition across wild Dhauladhar–Pir Panjal connecting terrain — for seasoned expedition teams only. From ₹27,999.
overview:
Chobia Pass Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. High Chobia Pass expedition across wild Dhauladhar–Pir Panjal connecting terrain — for seasoned expedition teams only.

Chobia is a high, committing crossing where Dhauladhar wildness meets Lahaul weather. Self-sufficiency and expedition leadership are non-negotiable.

The itinerary usually starts at Bharmour / Kugti side and ends at Lahaul side descent, spanning about 75 km across 8 day(s) with a high point near 16,700 ft. Graded difficult, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: July–September weather windows only; avoid deep winter

Flora along the Chobia Pass corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Chobia Pass Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Chobia Pass main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Chobia Pass camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Chobia Pass: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Bharmour–Lahaul a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Chobia Pass Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Kugti Pass Trek, Jalsu Pass Trek, Thamsar Pass Trek, Snowline Laka Trek.

Child policy for Chobia Pass Trek: 16+ experienced preferred. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Chobia Pass Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.
